teamObjective.explanation: This is the opening lead and my hand contains only low non-trump clubs. The priority is to begin shedding cards while preserving whatever modest card strength I have for later tricks.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) holds 3 unknown cards. No signals have been exchanged because the public history contains only the new_game event, so partner's strength and suit distribution must be treated as uniformly uncertain.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponent player 1 has only 2 cards remaining, which is public information from handCounts, so they are near finishing and may hold strong cards or a bomb. Opponents 1 and 3 each hold 3 cards; any of them could cover a low club single, but the exact cards are hidden.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of clubs is the safest opening play. It is the weakest legal card, minimizing the value exposed and keeping the 4, 5, and 6 available for later tricks. A low single also maximizes the chance that partner can play over it and take control of the lead.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the 6 first exposes my highest non-trump card. If an opponent covers it, I lose my best remaining card and keep only weaker cards, hurting future flexibility.
The 5 is still a middling card. Leading it now leaves the 3 and 4 as awkward low cards to shed later and does not materially increase the chance of winning the trick.
The 4 is slightly better than the 5 or 6, but the 3 is strictly lower and therefore gives up less information and less potential value, with no compensating advantage for leading the 4.

risks: The 3 of clubs is the lowest non-trump card and will likely be beaten by any higher single, so I will probably lose the lead.
Opponent player 1 has only 2 cards and may hold a strong combination or bomb, increasing the chance they seize control and finish soon.
Because all my cards are weak clubs, regaining control once it is lost will be difficult.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the 3 first, I retain my slightly stronger cards (4-6) for later and avoid revealing my higher cards prematurely. If partner can cover the lead, the team can continue the attack from a stronger position.
